top_improvements.title: Stop publishing pass_rate 1.0 from the keyless RubricJudge as a quality headline; relabel it 'determinism/regression smoke gate' and exclude it from any quality claim.
top_improvements.rationale: The 1.0 is computed by a judge that cannot fail the only hard cases, with those cases removed (golden.py:289-290, test_golden.py:179-182). Presenting it as quality evidence is the single most misleading artifact. Renaming it to what it actually guards (router/retrieval/citation-presence determinism) is honest and costs nothing.

top_improvements.title: Close the keyless number/negation-flip hole: do not auto-entail the >=0.8 overlap band — at minimum route numeric- or negation-bearing claims to the batched host judge regardless of overlap.
top_improvements.rationale: The host model is FREE on the keyless path (it costs host tokens, not $), so the cost argument for skipping the near-verbatim band (verifier.py:75-79) is weak precisely for the highest-risk claims (numbers, 'no/not'). A cheap regex gate ('does the claim contain a digit or a negation token that the span does not match?') before the overlap skip would catch the demonstrated 21%->12% and 'no risk' inversions.

top_improvements.title: Build the SPEC §201 injected-unsupported-claim recall harness and publish the real recall/precision number.
top_improvements.rationale: This is the one piece of credible, non-circular grounding evidence the project already promised (SPEC.md:201) but never built. Generate N reports, inject K unsupported/contradicted claims with known ground truth, run the verifier, report recall. That number — not a self-graded 1.0 — is what a third party would trust.

top_improvements.title: Replace the hardcoded-fail StubLLM 'semantic guard' tests with at least one recorded real-model transcript (cassette) per adversarial fixture.
top_improvements.rationale: test_golden.py:224-249 currently proves only that a stub returning 'fail' is parsed as fail. A single recorded real Claude judgment on fixtures 09/10 (committed as a fixture) would convert a circular test into actual evidence the host model catches the inversion.

top_improvements.title: Soften README/SPEC absolutes ('every claim ... verified', 'no hallucinations') to match the keyless reality.
top_improvements.rationale: The code is honest (golden.py:130-137); the marketing is not (README.md:17, SPEC.md:6). State plainly: keyless default = citation-presence + byte-identity + lexical-overlap support; entailment-grade contradiction-catching requires the [local] extra or a keyed judge. Aligning the two removes the only outright-bullshit-flagged claims.

verdict.competitor_notes: Against commercial Deep Research tools, the honest comparison is narrower than the marketing implies. Where bad-research genuinely wins: the deterministic uncited-claim gate (gate.py) and Tier-A byte-identity quote check are stronger and more transparent anti-fabrication primitives than what Gemini/OpenAI/Perplexity Deep Research expose — those tools cite but rarely guarantee the quoted span byte-matches the source, and never ship the gate logic for inspection. The contradiction-graph/source-tensions scaffold (SPEC §156) is also a real differentiator. Where it LOSES badly on the quality-evidence axis: OpenAI/Gemini/Anthropic publish (or are evaluated by third parties on) actual benchmark numbers and human ratings; bad-research's only emitted number is a self-graded, keyless 1.0 over cases hand-built to pass, with the two failing cases excluded. Grok/Gemini/OpenAI all run a strong model as the actual verifier in production; bad-research's default keyless verifier demonstrably passes a flipped number/negation (proven above), which a frontier-model judge in those systems would usually catch. So on raw structural grounding primitives it is competitive-to-better; on *demonstrated* end-to-end factual accuracy it has presented essentially no credible evidence, and one headline artifact is actively misleading. Net: better plumbing, unproven (and partly overstated) outcomes.
verdict.bottom_line: The quality is partly real and partly theater, and the line between them is sharp. Real: Tier-A byte-identity (verifier.py:144-151), the deterministic uncited-claim ship gate (gate.py:145-185), the recitation LCS (recitation.py:48-68), and the name-keyed NLI anti-inversion code (nli.py:59-91) are genuine, inspectable, non-circular engineering. Theater: the headline evidence. golden-eval-report.json's pass_rate 1.0 is self-graded by a judge that openly cannot detect contradiction (golden.py:130-137), computed over 8 cases after the only two cases that could fail are excluded as requires_llm (golden.py:289-290, test_golden.py:179-182). The 'semantic guard' that supposedly catches contradictions is tested only against a stub hardcoded to return 'fail' (conftest.py:74-86) — it proves plumbing, not detection. And on the DEFAULT keyless path the entailment check is a lexical-overlap shortcut that I empirically confirmed auto-passes a flipped number (21%->12%, overlap exactly 0.80) and a flipped negation ('20% increased risk'->'no increased risk', overlap 0.875) as SUPPORTED — directly contradicting README.md:17 'grounds every claim to a source' and SPEC.md:6 'no hallucinations (every claim is grounded + verified).' The real entailment lane that would catch these is opt-in and not installed by default. Credible third-party-grade evidence would require the injected-unsupported-claim recall harness the project already specced (SPEC.md:201) but never built, plus real recorded model judgments instead of fail-stubs. As-is, the foundations are better than average but the proof of quality is circular, and at least three load-bearing public claims are bullshit.

checks.claim: THE CENTRAL FINDING: on the default keyless ship path, the entailment check rubber-stamps number-flips and negation-flips. A claim with >=0.8 lexical overlap auto-returns ENTAILMENT with NO model call, even when a number or 'no' is inverted versus the cited span.
checks.evidence:
The CORE MECHANISM is confirmed against the real code. On the keyless ship path, cli/research.py:392 wires `default_nli(llm=llm)`, which (verifier.py:128-134, nli_available()=False when `[local]` absent) returns `LineSpanJudge`. LineSpanJudge.predict (verifier.py:106-113) returns `{entailment:1.0, neutral:0, contradiction:0}` with NO LLM call whenever `claim_quote_overlap(hypothesis, premise) >= CLAIM_QUOTE_OVERLAP_SKIP` (gate.py:206 = 0.8). classify_nli (nli.py:23-32) maps that to ENTAILMENT; verifier.py:386-387 records SUPPORTED; verifier.py:441-442 calls `store.set_verified(verified=1)`. The downstream ship-gate (gate.py:163-179) only flags when `anchor.verified != 1`, so a rubber-stamped flip passes ship silently. The code's OWN comments concede this: gate.py:198-205 ("a >=0.8-overlap report sentence that flipped a number ('grew 12%' vs a span saying 'grew 21%')... is a known keyless gap"), verifier.py:53-58, 76-78 ("residual number-flip risk in this band is the keyless gap"). nli.py:27's promise that "a quote that says the OPPOSITE is never silently passed" is indeed FALSE on this path: LineSpanJudge never emits a contradiction score, so the contradiction-first branch in classify_nli is dead on the keyless lane. I REPRODUCED a high-overlap number-flip passing: quote "Global EV sales rose 21 percent in 2023 according to the agency report" vs claim with "12 percent" -> overlap 0.90 -> ENTAILMENT, no model call. And "Inflation reached 8 percent..." vs "...3 percent..." -> 0.8889 -> ENTAILMENT. A single-word negation in a long sentence also passes: "the study found a 20% increased risk" vs "the study found no increased risk" -> exactly 0.80 -> ENTAILMENT.

WHERE THEY ARE WRONG (why partially-true, not confirmed): their two SPECIFIC short worked examples and their overlap numbers do NOT reproduce. claim_quote_overlap is asymmetric: |claim_content_tokens ∩ quote| / |claim_content_tokens| (gate.py:224-234), and the flipped token itself counts in the denominator AND is absent from the intersection, LOWERING overlap. Their example 1: quote "grew 21 percent" vs claim "grew 12 percent" -> content tokens {grew,12,percent} ∩ {grew,21,percent} = {grew,percent} = 2/3 = 0.6667, NOT their claimed 0.80. Routes to NEUTRAL (escalates to the batched host judge), NOT ENTAILMENT. Their example 2: "a 20% increased risk" vs "no increased risk" -> {no,increased,risk} ∩ {20,increased,risk} = 2/3 = 0.6667, NOT their claimed 0.875. Also NEUTRAL. So both of the auditor's headline examples and both of their cited overlap figures are factually incorrect; the flips in those exact short forms actually escalate to the host judge (test_host_judge.py:82-101 shows the NEUTRAL band is caught by the batched judge). The vulnerability is REAL but only bites when enough surrounding shared context dilutes the flipped token past 0.8 — i.e. it is phrasing-dependent, not the blanket auto-pass the framing implies, and short flips are caught. file:line citations gate.py:206 and verifier.py:441-444 are accurate; nli.py:27 quote is accurate.
checks.note: The mechanism, ship-path, and "no model call in the >=0.8 band" are fully verified and conceded by the code's own comments — this is a genuine, documented keyless soundness gap. But the auditor's two concrete examples and both overlap numbers (0.80, 0.875) are wrong; those exact short flips score 0.6667 and route to NEUTRAL (the host judge), not ENTAILMENT. The flaw requires longer, context-padded sentences to trigger. Severity is real but narrower than stated, and only on keyless+no-`[local]`; installing `[local]` (CrossEncoderNLI) or the Tier-C judge on the NEUTRAL band closes it. Tests test_host_judge.py:82-101 demonstrate the catch path works for genuine paraphrases/short flips, which is why "partially-true" rather than "confirmed."

checks.claim: The real entailment lane is dead code on the shipped machine: the [local] neural stack is not installed, so CrossEncoderNLI (the 0.70-floor, contradiction-checking lane described in SPEC §9) never runs by default.
checks.evidence:
Verified the entire chain against the real code/tests/docs:

1. Neural stack genuinely absent on this checkout. Ran importlib.util.find_spec: sentence_transformers -> None AND torch -> None. So the gate cannot open.

2. The auto-on gate. verifier.py:20-28 nli_available() returns importlib.util.find_spec("sentence_transformers") is not None (find_spec only, never imports torch). With it None, returns False.

3. The factory branch. verifier.py:128-134 default_nli(): `if nli_available(): return CrossEncoderNLI()` (line 128-131) is skipped; `if llm is not None: return LineSpanJudge(llm)` (132-133) else CitationPresentNLI() (134). So CrossEncoderNLI is unreachable without [local].

4. Which keyless fallback actually ships. The auditor said "LineSpanJudge, never CrossEncoderNLI" — confirmed precisely: the sole production call site research.py:392 builds CitationVerifier(nli=default_nli(llm=llm), ...) WITH a host llm, so the absent-stack default is LineSpanJudge (a lexical-overlap pre-filter -> host-token judge; verifier.py:106-113), not the cross-encoder. CitationPresentNLI is only the no-llm absolute fallback. Either way CrossEncoderNLI does not run.

5. Config + doctor cited lines verified verbatim. config.py:52 `neural_recall: bool = False  # opt-in local bi-encoder lane ([local])`. doctor.py:116 prints "local stack [dim]not installed (default: host-model rerank, FTS5/BM25 recall). `pip install 'bad-research[local]'` for offline neural." So "not installed" is the documented default state.

6. The lane the SPEC describes is real and is the 0.70/contradiction lane. SPEC.md:154 (under "## 9. Grounding" at SPEC.md:150) says CitationVerifier step (B) is "NLI entailment via local nli-deberta-v3-base ($0)". nli.py:11 NLI_MODEL_NAME = "nli-deberta-v3-base"; nli.py:13 ENTAILMENT_PASS = 0.70; nli.py:14 CONTRADICTION_FLAG = 0.50; CrossEncoderNLI.predict (nli.py:77-91) emits the entailment/contradiction/neutral softmax. So the "0.70-floor, contradiction-checking lane" the SPEC advertises is exactly CrossEncoderNLI, which is precisely the code path that never executes by default.

7. Tests codify this as intended ship behavior, not a bug: tests/test_grounding/test_verifier.py:200-213 test_default_nli_is_citation_present_stub_when_local_absent asserts that with [local] absent default_nli() is NOT a CrossEncoderNLI and the stub marks any premise as entailment>=0.70; lines 256-257 force nli_available->False and assert default_nli "must NOT import torch."
checks.note: Confirmed with one sharpening: in the actual CLI ship path the absent-stack default is LineSpanJudge (a keyless lexical-prefilter + host-token judge), not the pure CitationPresentNLI no-op, because research.py:392 threads a host llm in. The auditor's headline ("CrossEncoderNLI never runs by default") is fully correct; their parenthetical naming LineSpanJudge as the fallback is also correct for the CLI verify path. The defensible counter-framing the owner should weigh: this is an intentional, documented, tested opt-in design ("auto-on when [local] present"), not an accidental dead code path — and the keyless fallback is a real semantic check (host-judge over the paraphrase band), not a no-op, so "dead code" overstates it slightly. But the SPEC §9 prose at SPEC.md:154 presents "NLI entailment via local nli-deberta-v3-base" as the step-(B) behavior without flagging that it is opt-in and off by default, which is the legitimate honesty gap the weakness identifies.

checks.claim: Tier-A byte-identity is a genuine, non-circular $0 grounding check: it kills fabricated/drifted quotes by re-slicing the live note body at [char_start:char_end] AND verifying a SHA over the quote equals the anchor_id.
checks.evidence: VERIFIED AS REAL, DETERMINISTIC, NON-CIRCULAR, AND $0. tier_a_byte_identity (src/bad_research/grounding/verifier.py:144-151) returns False unless BOTH (1) quote_sha(anchor.quoted_support) == anchor.anchor_id AND (2) note_body[char_start:char_end] == anchor.quoted_support. quote_sha is an 8-char SHA-256 of the verbatim quote (src/bad_research/grounding/anchors.py:14-16); anchor_id defaults to that SHA (anchors.py:34-36). No model, no key, pure bytes. I ran the cited test test_tier_a_fails_on_sha_mismatch_fabricated_quote (tests/test_grounding/test_verifier.py:41-46) plus test_tier_a_passes... (32-38) and test_verify_fabricated_quote_tier_a_fails_unsupported (138-149): all 3 pass. The end-to-end fabricated-quote test asserts len(fake_llm.calls) == 0 (line 149) -- so the catch costs zero LLM tokens and is not self-graded. NON-CIRCULAR confirmed: note_bodies is read FRESH from disk at verify time (src/bad_research/cli/research.py:376-380, f.read_text over research/notes/*.md), so the slice is taken against the live fetched source note, not the model's own output or a self-rating. I reproduced all branches directly: drift (wrong offsets) -> False, forged anchor_id (sha mismatch) -> False, genuine match -> True.
checks.note: Core claim is correct, but the auditor's framing slightly overstates the SHA's role. Because anchor_id is auto-derived from quoted_support in ClaimAnchor.__post_init__ (anchors.py:34-36), in normal pipeline flow the SHA check and the slice check are NOT independent guards against quote fabrication/drift -- the slice equality (body[start:end] == quoted_support) is the actual workhorse that kills a drifted/fabricated quote; the SHA only fires independently when anchor_id is explicitly forged/corrupted (anchor-record tampering), which I had to construct by hand. Notably the auditor's own cited test (lines 41-46) exercises the SLICE failure, not a SHA-only failure -- its anchor_id is auto-derived and matches the quote's SHA, so the False verdict there comes entirely from the slice mismatch. The claim 'verifying a SHA ... equals the anchor_id' is literally true (the conjunction is real and both run), just imprecisely attributed: the SHA is an anchor-integrity/tamper check, and build_from_claims (anchors.py:152) deliberately stores the located body slice as quoted_support to keep the Tier-A round-trip holding, which means a rescued fuzzy-matched anchor still passes byte-identity. None of this undermines the $0 / deterministic / non-circular core, which is fully confirmed.

strengths.claim: The routing brain (router.py + routing_constants.py) is genuinely well-engineered: pure, side-effect-free, exhaustively unit-tested, with route/shape/effort cleanly separated as orthogonal axes.
strengths.evidence: 83 router tests pass (tests/test_skills/test_router*.py + test_plan_gate.py -> '83 passed in 0.04s'). router.py:269-313 classify_query_shape and plan_gate_fires both only READ classify_route, never mutate it (routing_constants.py:127-130 carries an explicit 'MUST NOT change classify_route' comment). Direct invocation matched docs: 17-item explicit-survey->fast, contradiction_terms->full, argumentative->full, time_periods->full. The B-5 modality gate (router.py:149-167) and SEMANTIC-TIERING floor (router.py:93-101) are real and tested.

strengths.claim: The compaction-resistance design rationale for splitting into per-step Skill() files is sound and self-aware, not cargo-cult.
strengths.evidence: bad-research.md:35 and :371-373 explain that one monolithic skill 'gets compacted away mid-run' and the orchestrator 'silently degrades'; loading each step fresh via Skill() at the moment of need is a defensible mitigation for a real LLM failure mode. The seven-piece subagent spawn contract (bad-research.md:267-285) names a concrete documented failure (a fetcher 'burns its whole budget searching for nonexistent sources' without stop_conditions) and pins output_shape to make step-11.5 binding deterministic. The user-global CLAUDE.md anti-hallucination guidance is also a real, evidence-based skill-craft practice.

strengths.claim: Skill-file consistency is partially machine-enforced: validate.py cross-checks that every Skill(skill: "...") reference resolves to a known skill, and the roster size is pinned by a test.
strengths.evidence: tests/test_skills/validate.py:31-53 (referenced_skills + 'unresolved skill reference' error); test_all_skills_valid.py asserts every roster entry has a file and test_step_skill_roster_is_exactly_18_full_tier_stages pins the count. test_light_critic.py:81 asserts the step-12 skill literally says '5 critics'. 25 validation tests pass. So broken Skill() wiring (the highest-risk failure) would be caught at CI.

strengths.claim: Individual step skills are tightly scoped with explicit exit criteria, recover-state sections, and disk-artifact handoffs — the coupling is via files on disk, not shared in-context state, which is the right pattern for a compaction-prone chain.
strengths.evidence: Every sampled step (decompose, fast, ultrafast, query-router, critics) has a 'Recover state' block listing exact input paths and an 'Exit criterion' block listing exact output artifacts (e.g. bad-research-1-decompose.md:205-211, bad-research-fast.md:161-166). The entry skill's recovery section (bad-research.md:296-316) maps each step to its canonical artifact so a compacted orchestrator can resume by finding the highest-numbered artifact on disk.

weaknesses.claim: A mandatory full-tier step the orchestrator invokes is never installed: bad-research-12.5-grader is in the entry skill as a Skill() call but absent from the install roster, so a real full run hits an unresolvable skill.
weaknesses.evidence: bad-research.md:126 invokes Skill(skill: "bad-research-12.5-grader") and :63 lists it as a mandatory full-tier stage. The file exists at src/bad_research/skills/bad-research-12.5-grader.md. But _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS (hooks.py) returns False for 'bad-research-12.5-grader' (confirmed via import), and _install_bad_research_step_skills iterates ONLY that roster (hooks.py:~3154). Worse, the prune loop deletes any non-roster bad-research-* step dir, so it cannot survive even from a prior install. The validate.py test misses this because its known_skills fixture globs all *.md on disk (conftest.py:14-15) rather than the install roster — it validates source files, not what ships.

weaknesses.claim: The entry skill's critic count is stale and self-contradicting: it says '4 adversarial critics' and its integrity gate checks only 4 critic-findings files, but step-12 and hooks.py define and spawn 5 (assumption critic added).
weaknesses.evidence: bad-research.md:49 ('4 adversarial critics'), :309 (recovery lists only critic-findings-{dialectic,depth,width,instruction}), :328-331 (integrity gate checks exactly those 4), :338 ('4-critic fan-out'). Contrast bad-research-12-critics.md:5,13,82,120 ('5 adversarial critics', 'all 5', spawns assumption-critic -> critic-findings-assumption.json) and hooks.py:1193 ASSUMPTION_CRITIC_AGENT + hooks.py:3163 _install_assumption_critic_agent. test_light_critic.py enforces '5 critics' in the STEP skill but NO test checks the entry skill's count, so the drift ships. The integrity gate will pass while silently never verifying the 5th critic ran.

weaknesses.claim: The entry skill repeatedly calls itself 'the 13 step skills' / '13 extra Skill invocations' — a stale number from a prior architecture; the real roster is 20 files / 18 full-tier stages.
weaknesses.evidence: bad-research.md:37 ('The 13 step skills'), :130, :229, :362 ('ALL 13 steps run'), :373 ('cost is 13 extra Skill invocations'). The roster has 20 entries (confirmed: len(_BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS)==20) and test_step_skill_roster_is_exactly_18_full_tier_stages pins 18 full-tier stages. The entry skill's OWN table (lines 39-66) lists ~19 rows. An orchestrator told 'ALL 13 steps run' for full while the table lists 18 stages is a genuine route-misfire risk — it may stop early thinking it is done.

weaknesses.claim: The entry skill is bloated with heavy restatement of the same routing conditions, inflating the always-loaded token cost.
weaknesses.evidence: bad-research.md is ~7.6K tokens loaded every run. The plan-gate skip conditions ('skipped on non-interactive / --auto / wrapped / small bounded run') are restated verbatim across lines 61, 75, 106, 108-117, 122, 254-256; 'plan-gate' appears 9 times and skip/no-op conditions 19 times in one file. The same fast-route sequence string is written out in full at :75, :92, and again in the route table. This is the bloat: the routing contract is repeated 3-4x in prose instead of once, which is exactly what makes the 4-vs-5-critic and 13-vs-18-step drift possible (more copies to keep in sync).

weaknesses.claim: The headline reliability claim '55.9 (full pipeline) vs 52.6 (single-draft fallback)' that justifies the entire multi-skill architecture has no traceable eval artifact — it cites only internal narrative docs.
weaknesses.evidence: Grep for 55.9/52.6 across the repo returns only docs/investigation/01_FOUNDATION.md, docs/superpowers/research/round1-current-state.md, and the self-referential bad-research.md:373. golden-eval-report.json (the only machine eval present) reports pass_rate 1.0 with every case rationale 'deterministic offline rubric' — a self-graded keyless rubric, not a head-to-head that could produce a 55.9-vs-52.6 delta. So the number used to defend the architecture's cost is asserted, not demonstrated.

weaknesses.claim: Identity/naming churn (bad-research vs hyperresearch) leaks throughout and is a maintainability hazard.
weaknesses.evidence: bad-research.md mixes the two: bootstrap uses `bad init`/`bad install` but step 0.5 archive uses `hyperresearch archive-run` (:189) and `$HPR` alias (:293); hooks.py agent prose says '7-phase hyperresearch pipeline' and 'Layer 1/2/3.5' (hooks.py:76,283,549) while the skills use '16/18-step' / numbered-step vocabulary. Two parallel mental models (Layers vs Steps, hyperresearch vs bad-research) coexist in the prompts a subagent reads, raising the odds of an agent following the wrong map.

top_improvements.title: Add bad-research-12.5-grader to _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS and add a test that the install roster (not just on-disk files) covers every Skill() reference in the entry skill.
top_improvements.rationale: This is a ship-breaking gap: the full route invokes an uninstalled skill. The fix is one roster line plus a guard test that diffs entry-skill Skill() targets against _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS — which would also have caught it.

top_improvements.title: Single-source the critic roster and step list: derive the entry skill's critic count, integrity-gate file list, and step count from one constant (or generate them), instead of hand-maintaining prose in 3+ places.
top_improvements.rationale: The 4-vs-5-critic and 13-vs-18-step drift exists purely because the same facts are restated in prose that no test checks. A generated/asserted table kills a whole class of route-misfire and false-pass-integrity-gate bugs.

top_improvements.title: Cut the entry skill's restatement bloat: state each routing/skip condition once, reference it by anchor elsewhere.
top_improvements.rationale: ~7.6K tokens load every single run and the plan-gate/skip conditions are repeated 4x. Tightening lowers per-run cost AND removes the duplicate copies that drift out of sync.

top_improvements.title: Either reproduce the 55.9/52.6 delta in golden-eval-report.json (or an honest head-to-head) or stop citing it as the architecture's justification.
top_improvements.rationale: The number that defends the expensive multi-skill design is unbacked by any eval artifact; a keyless self-graded rubric at pass_rate 1.0 cannot support it. Defend the design with a real measurement or with the (sound) compaction-resistance argument alone.

top_improvements.title: Unify the hyperresearch/bad-research and Layers/Steps vocabularies across skills and agent prose.
top_improvements.rationale: Subagents read agent prose (hooks.py) that talks 'Layer 5 / 7-phase hyperresearch' while the orchestrator talks 'step 12 / 18-stage bad-research'. One vocabulary reduces the chance an agent follows the wrong pipeline map.

verdict.competitor_notes: Versus commercial Deep Research (Gemini/OpenAI/Perplexity/Grok) this is an unusually transparent, auditable, keyless architecture — the per-step disk-artifact handoff and seven-piece spawn contract are real engineering those closed systems hide. Where it LOSES: the multi-skill chain's value claim rests on an unverifiable 55.9/52.6 number while the commercial tools publish (contestable but real) external benchmarks; and the ship-breaking 12.5-grader install gap plus 4-vs-5-critic drift are the kind of integration bugs a hosted product would have caught in prod. Versus a naive Claude+WebSearch baseline it clearly wins on grounding discipline (binding citations, uncited-gate, recitation-gate) and on not silently degrading mid-run — but a chunk of that win is process ceremony whose payoff is asserted, not measured.
verdict.bottom_line: The skill-craft is real, not theater: the routing engine is clean, orthogonal, and backed by 83 passing tests, and the per-step-Skill compaction-resistance design is a genuine, self-aware answer to a real LLM failure mode rather than ceremony. But the prompt-engineering layer is fragile in exactly the way over-restated prose always is. One ship-breaking defect (bad-research-12.5-grader is invoked on every full run yet omitted from the install roster, so the call cannot resolve) plus two live consistency drifts (entry skill says '4 critics' and a 4-file integrity gate while the pipeline runs 5; 'the 13 step skills' vs a 20-file/18-stage reality) mean the orchestrator is reading a map that no longer matches the territory. The tests guard the step skills and the Skill() wiring but not the entry skill's hand-maintained counts or the install roster's coverage of references — so the drift ships green. Net: a strong deterministic core wrapped in a bloated, under-tested orchestrator prompt that will misfire or hard-fail on a real full run until the grader install and the count drift are fixed.

checks.claim: A mandatory full-tier step the orchestrator invokes is never installed: bad-research-12.5-grader is in the entry skill as a Skill() call but absent from the install roster, so a real full run hits an unresolvable skill.
checks.evidence:
Every part verified, including runtime execution of the actual install function.

1) Entry skill invokes it as a mandatory full-tier stage. bad-research.md:126 = "- 12.5 → `Skill(skill: \"bad-research-12.5-grader\")` (in-pipeline grader loop... full only...)". It is listed as a full-tier half-step at bad-research.md:63, and appears in the canonical full sequence at bad-research.md:72 and the route table at :94 ("...12 → 13 → 12.5 → 14 ...| full"). The skill source file exists: src/bad_research/skills/bad-research-12.5-grader.md (11222 bytes), frontmatter name: bad-research-12.5-grader.

2) It is NOT in the install roster. hooks.py:3731-3752 defines _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS with exactly 20 entries; "bad-research-12.5-grader" is not one of them. Confirmed by import (PYTHONPATH=.../src): len==20, "bad-research-12.5-grader" in roster == False, no entry contains "grad".

3) Install iterates ONLY that roster. hooks.py:3775 `for skill_name in _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS:` is the only loop that writes SKILL.md files (hooks.py:3755 _install_bad_research_step_skills). Both install paths use it: the PreToolUse/global install (hooks.py:3154) and the lazy per-project `bad install --steps-only` path (cli/install.py:44). I RAN it on a fresh temp dir: 20 dirs created, grader SKILL.md exists == False.

4) The prune loop actively DELETES it even from a prior install. hooks.py:3795-3809 deletes any `bad-research-*` dir not in `expected` (the roster). I planted a bad-research-12.5-grader/SKILL.md from a 'prior install' and re-ran install: result message = "pruned: bad-research-12.5-grader"; the dir and file were removed. So it cannot survive.

5) The test that should catch this validates SOURCE files, not the ship roster. tests/test_skills/conftest.py:14-15 builds known_skills as `{p.stem for p in skills_dir.glob("bad-research*.md")} | {"bad-research"}`, where skills_dir (conftest.py:5) = src/bad_research/skills. test_grader_skill.py reads `skills_dir / "bad-research-12.5-grader.md"` (lines 8,14,25,34,41) and test_delegation_contract.py:35 asserts "bad-research-12.5-grader" in the entry-skill body — all assert on source on disk, never against _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS, so the gap is invisible to the suite.

6) No skip-if-missing guard. bad-research.md:362 = "RESPECT THE TIER GATE — never skip or add a step. For `full`, ALL 13 steps run... don't drop steps 'for budget.' The route is a binding contract." Step 12.5 has no conditional/skip clause, so a full run is contractually required to invoke the unresolvable skill.

Corroborating provenance: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-29-bad-research-super-skill.md:35 lists `core/hooks.py (roster)` among Workstream C edits, and install.py:6 docstring even claims the global install "ships ALL step skills" — it ships 20, omitting the grader.
checks.note: The auditor's specific citations were all accurate: bad-research.md:126 and :63, the roster behavior, conftest.py:14-15, and the prune loop. The auditor said "iterates ONLY that roster (hooks.py:~3154)" — 3154 is the install-step REGISTRATION callsite; the actual iteration is at hooks.py:3775 inside _install_bad_research_step_skills, which 3154 invokes. Minor line-pointer imprecision, substance correct. Practical severity is genuine but bounded: it only bites a real `full` route run (fast/ultrafast never invoke 12.5). On full, the host Skill tool would fail to resolve bad-research-12.5-grader — at best the LLM orchestrator improvises/skips the grader loop, at worst it errors. Either way the advertised "judge→patch→re-judge ≤3 convergence loop" does not actually run in a shipped full pipeline, and the grader-log.json / critic-findings-grader.json artifacts (bad-research.md:311) would never be produced.

checks.claim: The entry skill's critic count is stale and self-contradicting: it says "4 adversarial critics" and its integrity gate checks only 4 critic-findings files, but step-12 and hooks.py define and spawn 5 (assumption critic added).
checks.evidence:
Every cited line verified verbatim. ENTRY SKILL (src/bad_research/skills/bad-research.md) is stuck at 4: line 49 "4 adversarial critics in parallel → findings JSONs"; line 309 recovery map "Step 12: research/critic-findings-{dialectic,depth,width,instruction}.json" (4 only); lines 328-331 integrity gate `for f in ...` tests exactly critic-findings-{dialectic,depth,width,instruction}.json (4 only); line 338 "(The fast route skips the full 4-critic fan-out...". I additionally grepped the whole entry skill: it contains ZERO occurrences of "assumption", "5 critics", "all 5", or "5 adversarial". So the drift is consistent and total across the entry skill.

STEP SKILL (bad-research-12-critics.md) is at 5: line 5 description "spawns 5 adversarial critics in parallel"; line 13 "full tier → spawn all 5 critics"; line 82 "Spawn all 5 critics in parallel"; lines 87-89 explicitly add bad-research-assumption-critic → research/critic-findings-assumption.json; line 120 exit criterion "All 5 critic findings JSONs exist".

HOOKS (src/bad_research/core/hooks.py): line 1193 ASSUMPTION_CRITIC_AGENT defined (output_path research/critic-findings-assumption.json at line 1227); _install_assumption_critic_agent installed at lines 3163 (project) and 3219 (global), defined at line 3508, writing bad-research-assumption-critic.md (line 3513).

DOWNSTREAM CONSUMES THE 5TH (so the gate omission is materially wrong, not cosmetic): patcher bad-research-14-patcher.md:38 reads a files list INCLUDING 'research/critic-findings-assumption.json', and :87 lists it again ("full tier only; B-1 assumption critic"). So the real full-tier pipeline produces and uses 5 findings files; only the entry skill's gate/recovery/prose lag at 4.

TEST COVERAGE GAP CONFIRMED: tests/test_skills/test_light_critic.py:68-81 (test_critics_skill_keeps_full_tier_four_critic_path) and :137-150 assert "5 critics"/"all 5"/assumption-critic ONLY against bad-research-12-critics.md (the STEP skill, loaded via _critics_skill()/skills_dir). I grepped tests/test_skills/test_entry_skill.py for critic/integrity/critic-findings/assumption: ZERO matches. So no test pins the entry skill's critic count or its integrity-gate file list — the 4-vs-5 drift ships unguarded.

CONSEQUENCE as the auditor stated: the integrity gate at bad-research.md:328-335 will pass (all 4 listed files present) while NEVER verifying critic-findings-assumption.json was produced, so a silently-skipped 5th critic goes undetected on the full route.
checks.note: The defect is real but low-severity in operational terms. The entry-skill integrity gate is a soft `test -f ... || echo MISSING` advisory loop (no exit code / no hard fail), so even for the 4 files it tracks it only prints warnings rather than blocking ship. The actual fan-out and patcher consumption are driven by the step-12 and step-14 skills (correctly at 5), so a normal run does spawn the assumption critic; the entry skill is documentation/gate drift, not a missing critic. Net: confirmed self-contradiction and confirmed coverage hole, but the "5th critic silently never runs" risk only materializes on a resume/partial run that leans on the stale entry-skill gate, not on a clean full run.

checks.claim: The routing brain (router.py + routing_constants.py) is genuinely well-engineered: pure, side-effect-free, exhaustively unit-tested, with route/shape/effort cleanly separated as orthogonal axes.
checks.evidence:
Verified all sub-claims against the actual code, tests, and live invocation.

PURITY / SIDE-EFFECT-FREE (confirmed): router.py and routing_constants.py contain ZERO I/O — no open(), os, httpx/requests, logging, print, or global mutation (grep returned only comment/docstring hits). The only import in router.py is `routing_constants as R` (router.py:32). Live test: calling classify_route, classify_query_shape, plan_gate_fires, route_reason, shape_fanout on a dict left the input byte-identical (JSON before==after). shape_fanout (router.py:338 `dict(R.SHAPE_FANOUT[shape])`) and effort_overrides (router.py:356 `dict(R.EFFORT_MAP[effort])`) return defensive COPIES — I mutated the returned dicts and the module constants stayed untouched. So the functions are genuinely pure over dicts + frozen constants.

EXHAUSTIVELY UNIT-TESTED (confirmed, count slightly off): the auditor said 83; the actual collected count across tests/test_skills/test_router.py(12) + test_router_effort.py(15) + test_router_modality.py(15) + test_router_shape.py(20) + test_router_skill.py(2) + test_router_tiering.py(12) + test_plan_gate.py(9) = 85, and `pytest ... -o addopts=""` reports `85 passed in 0.06s`. Their `83 passed in 0.04s` is a minor undercount (likely 2 tests added since their run, or test_router_skill excluded), not a fabrication. The body of work is real and green.

ROUTE/SHAPE ORTHOGONALITY (confirmed): classify_route (router.py:183-200) reads ONLY decompose fields — it never calls classify_query_shape or effort_overrides. classify_query_shape (router.py:269-313) and plan_gate_fires (219-259) call classify_route only as a READ (lines 255, 293 indirectly), never mutating route. grep confirms classify_query_shape is invoked only by shape_reason (319) and shape_fanout (337). routing_constants.py:127-130 carries the explicit comment "query_shape ... MUST NOT change classify_route's fast/full decision ... never feeds back into the route" (their 127-130 cite is exact). Live sweep proved independence: route=full/shape=depth_first, route=fast/shape=breadth_first, route=fast/shape=straightforward all co-occur.

CITED LINE RANGES (confirmed): _breadth_forces_full (the B-5 modality gate) is exactly router.py:149-167; _pipeline_tier_floor_full (SEMANTIC-TIERING floor) is exactly router.py:93-101. Both as cited.

DOCS-MATCHING BEHAVIOR (confirmed by direct invocation): 17-item EXPLICIT survey (modality="survey")->fast; contradiction_terms->full; argumentative->full; time_periods->full; multi-domain(3)->full; pipeline_tier="full"->full; and the B-5 fix is real: a 17-item LEXICAL survey with NO explicit modality field ->full (detect_modality still infers "survey" but _explicit_breadth_modality gates the down-route on the explicit field). Every documented branch behaves as claimed.
checks.note: One caveat on "orthogonal axes": route and shape ARE truly orthogonal, but effort is a manual OVERRIDE, not a fully independent axis — effort_overrides pins `route` (minimal/low->fast, medium/high->full, routing_constants.py:184-193), so the --effort dial REPLACES the auto-classified route rather than running beside it. The claim's "three orthogonal axes" framing is accurate for route vs shape and defensible for effort-as-config, but effort technically overrides route rather than being orthogonal to it. Minor; does not undercut the strength. Also: this verifies the routing LOGIC is sound, not that the routes do useful work downstream — the 85 tests are unit tests of a pure decision tree, separate from the keyless/self-graded-eval concerns elsewhere in the repo.

verdict.lens: Operator / real-user honesty: would a real person choose this over clicking "Deep Research" in ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity?

strengths.claim: For an existing Claude Code user the marginal setup cost is genuinely tiny and the keyless story is real, not marketing.
strengths.evidence: README.md:24-36 — full setup is `pipx install bad-research` + `bad install` + `bad doctor`. install.py:51-53 — default global install writes entry skill + hook so `/bad-research` works in every session. doctor.py:85-87 prints 'keyless by default — zero third-party API key required'; inference rides the host model the user already pays for. Defensible vs Perplexity/ChatGPT/Gemini DR, each of which needs its own paid subscription.

strengths.claim: The persistent local vault is a real differentiator no hosted DR product offers: every fetched source becomes a reusable local note that compounds across runs and lives on the user's disk.
strengths.evidence: README.md:56-57 'every fetched source land in a vault under ./research/ that compounds across sessions'; bad-research.md:184 describes the SQLite vault as 'every fetched source becomes a note here'. ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity throw away their working set after each run; here the corpus is greppable and owned. This is the single most honest reason to prefer it.

strengths.claim: A genuine three-tier ladder exists in code, so the tool can in principle be fast when the question is small — the fast route really is a bounded loop, not a crippled full run.
strengths.evidence: router.py:183-200 classify_route returns 'fast' for anything not multi-domain/contested/broad; bad-research-fast.md:13-21 caps it at FAST_MAX_STEPS=6 with a <10-min target and an auditable stop rule. bad-research-ultrafast.md:18-23 adds a 5-15 min middle tier. The plumbing for 'right-sized effort' is real.

strengths.claim: Output is plain local Markdown the user owns, not locked inside a web app — directly editable, diffable, and pipeable.
strengths.evidence: bad-research-fast.md:159 and bad-research-ultrafast.md:89 both write to research/notes/final_report_<vault_tag>.md. For an operator who wants the report in their repo/editor rather than a chat transcript, this beats copy-pasting out of a hosted UI.

weaknesses.claim: The default path silently drops the user into the 1.5-2.5h full pipeline, and the README advertises no way to avoid it. The exact behavior the README encourages ('just ask a research-shaped question') routes broad/contested queries to full automatically.
weaknesses.evidence: routing_constants.py:189-190 — default effort 'medium' maps route:'full'. router.py:191-195 — classify_route returns 'full' for multi_domain OR contested OR breadth OR a pipeline_tier='full' floor, and the floor is honored as an escalate-only gate (router.py:7-18). README.md:51-56 tells users to 'let Claude trigger it' with 'literature review', 'comparing vector databases' — all of which are multi-domain/breadth triggers. The README never mentions --effort, --fast, or --ultrafast (grep of README returns zero hits). So a real user asking a normal research question gets a 1.5-2.5h run with no advertised escape hatch.

weaknesses.claim: There is no operator-facing progress UI for a multi-hour run. The user watches a raw Claude Code agent transcript, not a Deep-Research-style status panel — and there is no spinner/ETA/percent-complete anywhere in the codebase.
weaknesses.evidence: grep for progress|spinner|status bar|live update|streaming across the entry skill + all cli/*.py returned nothing. bad-research.md:367 even forbids bare text while tasks run ('NEVER EMIT BARE TEXT WHILE TASKS ARE RUNNING') and routes thoughts to research/temp/orchestrator-notes.md, so the user sees tool spam, not a clean ETA. Hosted DR products show a live research plan + sources-found counter; this shows none of that. For a 1.5-2.5h wait that is a major UX gap.

weaknesses.claim: The headline 'pass_rate 1.0' eval is self-graded and offline, so it gives a prospective user zero real signal that the output beats a hosted DR product. Every case verdict is the same canned string.
weaknesses.evidence: golden-eval-report.json:1 pass_rate 1.0 over 8 cases; every single case rationale (lines 23, 47, 71, etc.) is the literal string 'deterministic offline rubric'. A keyless tool self-grading its own output 8/8 with an offline rubric is circular — it tells an operator nothing about whether the report is better than Perplexity's. The owner asked 'is any of it bullshit?': this eval, as a quality signal, is theater.

weaknesses.claim: It is strictly worse than hosted DR for the most common real use case — a quick, decent, one-off answer — because of cold-start friction and the wait. There is no mobile, no share link, no 'good enough in 3 minutes from my phone' path.
weaknesses.evidence: To get an answer the user must be at a terminal with Claude Code open, in a project dir, and either accept whatever route classify_route (router.py:183-200) picks or know the undocumented --effort minimal flag. Bootstrap (bad-research.md:183-263) runs vault init + lazy step-skill install + archive-run + tag minting BEFORE step 1 even starts. Clicking 'Deep Research' in ChatGPT is one button and a phone. For the median knowledge-worker query, this is more setup for a slower, uglier result.

weaknesses.claim: The middle tier (ultrafast, 5-15 min) — the one band that would actually compete head-to-head with hosted DR on speed-vs-depth — is hidden behind an undocumented flag and never auto-selected, so almost no real user will ever hit it.
weaknesses.evidence: bad-research.md:96-104 — ultrafast 'is never auto-selected'; only the --ultrafast flag or a literal 'ultrafast mode' phrase triggers it. router.py:34 + classify_route only ever emit fast/full (router.py:195,200). The README mentions ultrafast zero times. The most competitive tier is effectively dark.

top_improvements.title: Show the chosen route + a one-line ETA and let the user confirm/downgrade BEFORE a long run starts
top_improvements.rationale: The single biggest operator failure is being silently committed to a 1.5-2.5h run. The plan-gate (bad-research-1.6-plan-gate) already exists and fires on interactive full runs (bad-research.md:108-117) — but it gates the *plan*, not the *time cost*, and is skipped on small runs. Surfacing 'Route: full · est. 1.5-2.5h · type --fast to shorten' at the top of every interactive run, derived from the already-computed route (router.py classify_route) and EFFORT_MAP, converts the worst surprise into an informed choice. Low effort because the route and time band are already known at step 1.5.

top_improvements.title: Document --effort / --fast / --ultrafast in the README 'Use it' section and recommend ultrafast as the default 'try it' tier
top_improvements.rationale: The README actively steers users toward the slow path and hides the two fast paths entirely (grep of README: zero hits for effort/fast/ultrafast). The 5-15 min ultrafast tier (bad-research-ultrafast.md:18) is the genuinely competitive offering vs hosted DR, yet it is dark. A three-line table in README.md plus a 'fastest way to try it: /bad-research --ultrafast <q>' example would widen appeal more than any code change, at near-zero cost.

top_improvements.title: Add a lightweight live progress line (route, current step N/total, sources-found count) to the orchestrator-notes the user already sees
top_improvements.rationale: Hosted DR products win the wait emotionally with a visible plan + sources counter. This codebase already tracks vault count and step todos (bad-research.md:295,367) but renders none of it as operator-facing status. A single periodic 'Step 5/16 · 23 sources · ~40 min remaining' line would make the multi-hour wait tolerable. Medium effort because it needs a clean status seam rather than the temp-notes hack.

top_improvements.title: Replace or supplement the self-graded golden-eval with a real head-to-head vs a hosted DR product on a handful of public questions
top_improvements.rationale: pass_rate 1.0 with rationale 'deterministic offline rubric' on all 8 cases (golden-eval-report.json) is circular and gives a prospective operator no reason to trust the output over Perplexity. Even 5 side-by-side comparisons (same question, both tools, blind human or third-model judging) would do more to justify the time/setup cost than the entire current eval suite.

verdict.bottom_line: Honestly: the real user is a narrow but real audience — a Claude-Code-native developer/researcher who wants a fully-cited report as owned local Markdown, with a persistent searchable source vault, and who refuses to pay for yet another DR subscription. For that person it genuinely wins on cost (keyless, README.md:24-36), on data ownership (the compounding ./research/ vault, README.md:56-57), and on output portability (plain Markdown files). That is a defensible, if small, niche. For everyone else it is strictly worse than clicking 'Deep Research' in a hosted product: more cold-start friction (pipx + bad install + a terminal + a project dir + bootstrap), no progress UI for a multi-hour wait (zero progress/spinner code exists), no mobile/share path, and a self-graded 8/8 eval that proves nothing about output quality. The deepest problem is positioning, not engineering: the README steers users toward the 1.5-2.5h full pipeline (default effort 'medium' = full, routing_constants.py:189) while hiding the two faster tiers that would actually compete (--fast and the 5-15 min --ultrafast appear zero times in the README), and it never warns the user before committing them to hours. The single highest-leverage UX change is also the cheapest: surface the chosen route + ETA and let the user downgrade before the long run begins, and document the fast tiers — that alone would move this from 'great for a niche' toward 'a real alternative for a wide audience.'
verdict.competitor_notes: Wins vs all hosted DR (ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity/Grok DR): keyless/no extra subscription, a persistent local source vault that compounds across runs (none of the hosted tools keep your corpus), and output as owned editable Markdown. Loses badly to all of them on: one-click/mobile access, a polished live research-progress UI with a visible plan and sources-found counter, share links, and time-to-first-answer for a median query (one button vs terminal+bootstrap+possible 1.5-2.5h). Where it could draw level — the 5-15 min ultrafast middle tier (bad-research-ultrafast.md:18) is the only band engineered to match hosted DR's speed-vs-depth tradeoff — it forfeits the matchup by hiding ultrafast behind an undocumented, never-auto-selected flag (bad-research.md:96-104). vs Perplexity DR specifically: Perplexity is far faster and needs zero setup; bad-research only beats it on cost-and-ownership for someone already living in Claude Code. Net: it does NOT beat hosted DR for the average operator today, but it occupies a real, defensible corner (keyless + owned vault + local Markdown) that none of them serve.

checks.claim: The default path silently drops the user into the 1.5-2.5h full pipeline, and the README advertises no way to avoid it; the README-encouraged 'just ask a research-shaped question' behavior routes broad/contested queries to full automatically. (Cited evidence: routing_constants.py:189-190 default effort 'medium' maps route:'full'; router.py:191-195 classify_route returns full for multi_domain OR contested OR breadth OR pipeline_tier='full' floor; README:51-56 examples are all multi-domain/breadth triggers; README has zero hits for --effort/--fast/--ultrafast.)
checks.evidence:
CONFIRMED — no advertised escape hatch: grep of README.md for `--effort|--fast|--full|--ultrafast|--auto|override|escape` returns ZERO hits. README.md:55-56 only says it auto-"scales to the question," naming the "~1.5-2.5 h" full pipeline for "broad or contested" queries, with no user-facing way to pin a cheaper route. The levers exist but are undocumented in the README (research.py:29-37 defines --fast/--full/--ultrafast on the internal `bad route` cmd; bad-research.md:135-153 documents --effort). CONFIRMED bias-to-full mechanism, but NOT the one cited: the real bias is pipeline_tier defaulting to "full" — bad-research-1-decompose.md:139 "Default is `full`. When uncertain, tier up" — which classify_route honors as a one-way escalate-only floor (router.py:101 _pipeline_tier_floor_full, used at router.py:192).

REFUTED — the auditor's PRIMARY cited mechanism is a misread. routing_constants.py:189-190 is the EFFORT_MAP "medium" entry whose `"tier":"default"` is a label, not a global-default declaration. The live default run NEVER consults EFFORT_MAP: research.py:250 `effort: str = typer.Option(None, "--effort")` defaults to None; research.py:271 calls effort_overrides(effort), and router.py:354-356 returns None for None/invalid effort, leaving classify_route's auto-route untouched. So "default effort medium maps route full" does not govern a normal run.

REFUTED — "README examples all route to full." I ran classify_route on the project's own golden corpus (PYTHONPATH=src): 02_comparison "How do PostgreSQL and MySQL differ in default isolation level?" -> fast (4 atomic items, no trigger); 07_breadth_list -> fast; 05_definitional -> fast; 08_numeric_precise -> fast. Only 03_multidomain_full (>=3 domains) and 04_contested_argumentative (argumentative + contradiction_terms) -> full. A two-entity comparison routes FAST, directly contradicting "comparing vector databases routes full automatically." router.py:191-195 verified verbatim: full only if pipeline_tier floor OR time_periods OR argumentative OR contradiction OR multi_domain(>=3) OR breadth-over-ceiling — and whether a README query trips these depends on the MODEL's decompose output, not a mechanical default.
checks.note: Net: the user-facing concern (README hides every route lever and frames full as the automatic outcome) is real and worth fixing. But the auditor's mechanical chain is half-wrong: the route is decided by classify_route + the model-set pipeline_tier default, not by an EFFORT_MAP "medium" default (which is inert unless --effort is explicitly passed), and the project's own golden cases show comparison/definitional/breadth-list queries route FAST, not full. So a "normal research question" does NOT uniformly land in the 1.5-2.5h pipeline; only genuinely multi-domain/contested/argumentative ones (or ones the model itself tiers full) do. The weakness is best restated as: (1) README documents no way to pick/cap the route, and (2) the decompose step's pipeline_tier defaults to full, biasing toward the expensive path — not as a mechanical effort=medium default.

checks.claim: There is no operator-facing progress UI for a multi-hour run. The user watches a raw Claude Code agent transcript, not a Deep-Research-style status panel — and there is no spinner/ETA/percent-complete anywhere in the codebase.
checks.evidence:
VERIFIED INDEPENDENTLY — claim holds on every point.

1) No progress UI in code. A broad grep for progress|spinner|ETA|percent-complete|sources-found|status panel|live update|ProgressEvent|NDJSON|SSE|tqdm|indicatif|halo across src/bad_research/ returns ZERO real hits — the only matches are unrelated identifiers (e.g. research.py:128 `searxng_endpoint`, fetch.py:47 `media`, getattr accessors). `rich>=13` IS a declared dep (pyproject.toml:46), but `grep "rich\.|Progress|track(|Console"` across src/ shows it is used ONLY in cli/_output.py:14-16 (a JSON-line emitter) and cli/doctor.py:83-116 (a STATIC keyless-capability table). There is no `rich.progress`, no spinner, no `track()`, no ETA anywhere. cli/research.py (the main research entrypoint) renders no progress at all.

2) The auditor's cited line is exact. bad-research.md:367 reads verbatim: "14. **NEVER EMIT BARE TEXT WHILE TASKS ARE RUNNING.** ... Every response while subagent tasks are in flight MUST include a tool call; the best one is appending analytical thoughts to `research/temp/orchestrator-notes.md`." So the orchestrator's between-step thinking is routed to a temp file, not surfaced as a clean status line — the user sees the raw tool-call transcript. Corroborated at bad-research.md:19 ("Between steps, do nothing except mark todos and (optionally) think to research/temp/orchestrator-notes.md").

3) The design docs ADMIT this is unimplemented and deliberately deferred. docs/investigation/06_CROSSCUTTING_PACKAGING.md:255-263 (§"A5 — Streaming / Progress Protocol") states plainly: "As a Claude Code skill, *progress streaming is the host's job*... The TodoWrite list doubles as the user-visible progress bar... There is **no SSE/WebSocket** in the package." The richer "live progress feed (which stage, which subagent, how many sources fetched, current spend)" with a `ProgressEvent` schema + Rich/ora UI (06:269-285) is explicitly labeled "(IDEA)" / "Recommendation", and docs/investigation/09_SPEED_COST_CURATION.md:456 marks "ProgressEvent schema → NDJSON(CLI) / SSE(server) dual transport" as **DEFER** ("v1 needs no SSE"). So a Deep-Research-style status panel was scoped out, not built.
checks.note: Minor nuance, not a refutation: the project's stated stance is that the Claude Code host's own TUI + TodoWrite checklist IS the progress surface (06:259-260), so "the user watches a raw agent transcript" is by design rather than an oversight. But the auditor's substantive points — no in-package spinner/ETA/percent-complete, no DR-style sources-found counter, thoughts hidden in a temp file, and a 1.5-2.5h full run with no purpose-built progress panel — are all factually correct. top_improvements.title: Spike the unknowns BEFORE writing any installer: confirm spawn_agent signature, whether Codex auto-discovers ~/.codex/skills/ by directory, and whether [features] multi_agent is a real flag
top_improvements.rationale: The entire target layout and dispatch model are guesses (spec lines 156-159, 215-221; no spawn_agent/multi_agent reference exists outside the spec). If Codex does not discover skills by directory name, or spawn_agent has a different signature, the install target and D1 parity are both invalid. A half-day of reading current Codex docs + one manual smoke (drop a trivial SKILL.md in ~/.codex/skills/, see if it triggers; try one spawn_agent) de-risks the whole project. Do this first or risk building the wrong thing.

top_improvements.title: Fix the subagent-dispatch translation: map `subagent_type: bad-research-X` (colon form, multi-line) to an explicit 'read references/agents/X.md and pass it inline to spawn_agent' instruction
top_improvements.rationale: The plan's literal sub `Task(subagent_type=` never matches the real colon-form YAML syntax used across ~15 skill files (verified via grep). The bare-token fallback produces `agent: bad-research-X` with no pointer to the prompt file — so spawn_agent fires with no prompt. This is the load-bearing mechanism of the whole port (D1 full parity) and it is broken as designed. Needs a name→reference-path resolver applied to the colon form, plus a test asserting each translated subagent_type line yields a 'read references/agents/<name>.md' instruction.

top_improvements.title: Correct the agent roster to 17 (add assumption-critic) and the step roster to read ONLY from _BAD_RESEARCH_STEP_SKILLS + the entry skill, dropping the stale spec roster (3-contradiction-graph / 7-source-tensions / 9-evidence-digest don't exist)
top_improvements.rationale: Building to the spec's 16-agent / mis-named-step roster ships a pipeline missing the assumption-critic (one of 5 step-12 critics, installed on Claude at hooks.py:3219) and attempts to read 3 non-existent step files. The plan partially self-corrects by reading the live roster constant, but the dispatch-table.md asset still hardcodes 4 critics and omits 12.5-grader. Reconcile both rosters against hooks.py ground truth and add a roster-completeness test that diffs the rendered agent set against the 17 *_AGENT constants.

top_improvements.title: Excise (don't just verbally counter) the Claude-only bootstrap gate when rendering SKILL.md: strip the '.claude/skills/... steps-only' check and the 'bootstrap must be done before step 1' sentence, since references/ ship the procedures inline
top_improvements.rationale: The entry skill body (bad-research.md:185, 379-385) hard-gates step 1 behind a per-project step-skill install that does not exist on Codex. A preamble saying 'ignore it' leaves contradictory instructions in one file. Add targeted removal rules (or a small Codex router-section override, which D4 already reserves) so the rendered SKILL.md has no .claude/ path references and no mandatory-bootstrap gate. This also makes the plan's leak-lint (Task 9) pass honestly instead of via path-mangling substitutions.

top_improvements.title: Then execute the plan's Task 1-10 sequence as written (translation module → installer → openai.yaml/AGENTS.md/config → leak-lint + idempotency tests → manual smoke), with the fixes above folded in
top_improvements.rationale: The TDD task breakdown is genuinely solid scaffolding — real failing tests, real minimal implementations, idempotency + leak-lint guards. Once the four correctness bugs above are fixed and the unknowns confirmed, the remaining work is mechanical. Rough estimate for a competent dev: 0.5 day spike + ~2-3 days implementation/tests + 0.5 day manual Codex smoke ≈ 3-4 days to a working light-tier run; full-tier subagent fan-out verification adds risk if spawn_agent parallelism differs from Task.

verdict.competitor_notes: This lens is about portability of the owner's own tool, not head-to-head research quality, so direct DR-tool comparison is tangential. The relevant competitive point: Grok/Gemini/OpenAI/Perplexity Deep Research are single-vendor hosted services with no cross-harness install story at all — you cannot run them inside Codex OR Claude Code. bad-research's keyless, host-model design is genuinely differentiated precisely because it CAN target multiple agent harnesses from one Python core. That portability is a real moat the commercial DR products structurally cannot match. But it only counts once the Codex path actually ships; today it is a spec, so the 'works in both' claim is aspirational, not delivered.
verdict.bottom_line: The Codex-port spec gets the big call right — most of the system is platform-neutral Python behind a shell, and only the install surface plus tool vocabulary need porting — and the implementation plan is unusually concrete (real TDD tasks, idempotency and leak-lint guards, single-source transform that reads the live skill/agent constants). That earns it more than a 'theater' verdict. But it is not yet sound enough to build to without rework, and it is 0% implemented. Three hard problems: (1) the subagent-dispatch translation, the literal point of 'full parity', doesn't match the real `subagent_type:` colon syntax and would dispatch agents with no prompt; (2) the roster is miscounted — 17 agents not 16, with the assumption-critic dropped, and the spec lists three step files that no longer exist; (3) the foundational Codex primitives (spawn_agent, multi_agent, skill auto-discovery) are presented as settled but exist nowhere outside the spec and are explicitly flagged as unconfirmed. The minimal real path to 'installable via Codex': a half-day spike to confirm the primitives, fix the dispatch/roster/bootstrap bugs, then run the plan's tasks — roughly 3-4 days to a verified light-tier run. The plan is a good skeleton built on partly-stale anatomy and unverified physics; usable, but only after a correctness pass and a docs spike.

checks.claim: The codex-port subagent-dispatch translation (the point of D1 "full parity") does not work: real skills dispatch via colon-form `subagent_type: bad-research-X`, but the plan's literal sub targets paren-form `Task(subagent_type=` (never matches); the fallback `('subagent_type','agent')` rewrites to `agent: bad-research-X` which does NOT resolve to `references/agents/X.md`, leaving the orchestrator with a bare agent name and no instruction to read the prompt file, so subagents get empty/guessed prompts.
checks.evidence:
The MECHANICAL premises are confirmed, but the load-bearing CONCLUSION ("empty/guessed prompts") is refuted by the plan's own design.

CONFIRMED parts:
1. Real dispatch is colon-form. `grep subagent_type src/bad_research/skills/*.md` = 15 hits across 12 files, all `subagent_type: bad-research-<X>` (e.g. bad-research-10-triple-draft.md:245 draft-orchestrator; bad-research-4-loci-analysis.md:81 loci-analyst; bad-research-5-depth-investigation.md:45 depth-investigator).
2. The paren-form target is dead. `grep "Task(subagent_type"` and `grep "Task(.*subagent_type"` both return NOTHING (exit 1). Stronger than the auditor said: the two literal `Task(` blocks (bad-research-1-decompose.md:38, bad-research-6-cross-locus-reconcile.md:86) use a `prompt:`-only form with NO subagent_type at all. So _LITERAL_SUBS[0] `("Task(subagent_type=","spawn_agent(agent=")` (plan docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-29-codex-port.md:171) matches zero bytes of the corpus.
3. The fallback `("subagent_type","agent")` (plan:175) does rewrite `subagent_type: bad-research-draft-orchestrator` -> `agent: bad-research-draft-orchestrator` as a pure string replace.

REFUTED conclusion ("bare name, no read instruction, empty/guessed prompts"):
- The plan injects a Codex router-preamble (plan:365-373) that EXPLICITLY tells the orchestrator: "Subagents are dispatched with spawn_agent. Each subagent's prompt lives in `references/agents/<name>.md`. To dispatch one: read that file, append the per-call inputs... See `references/dispatch-table.md`." Plus a full stage->agent-file dispatch table (plan:400-409). So name->file resolution is handled by the prepended preamble + dispatch table, NOT by the substitution table. The auditor assumed the literal table alone must do the resolution; it was never designed to.
- The agent prompt bodies are real and get written: `_build_agent_files` extracts the 16 agent prompts from hooks.py constants (hooks.py:55 loci-analyst, :255 depth-investigator, etc.) into `references/agents/<name>.md` (plan:503,636-638).
- Every colon-form dispatch in the skills is immediately followed by an inline `prompt: |` block carrying the full per-call instructions (grep -A1 confirms all 15). The translator preserves that inline prompt verbatim, so the orchestrator is NOT left with "empty/guessed prompts" even before reading the agent file.

CONTEXT: All of this is UNIMPLEMENTED. No codex_translate.py / codex_install.py exist (find src -iname '*codex*' = empty), no codex tests, no --codex flag in cli/install.py. So nothing "does not work as specified" at runtime — it doesn't run at all; the critique is against a paper plan.
checks.note: Verdict is partially-true: the auditor correctly proved the `Task(subagent_type=` literal is a no-op against the actual colon-form corpus (a genuine sloppiness in the plan's substitution table). But the central claim that this leaves subagents with empty/guessed prompts is wrong - the plan resolves name->prompt-file via the injected router preamble (plan:365-373) and dispatch-table (plan:400-409), and preserves the inline `prompt: |` blocks. The auditor wrongly assumed the literal-sub table was the sole resolution mechanism. Also note the whole codex port is unimplemented (no source/tests/flag), so this is a design critique of a spec, not of working code. The auditor cited `plans/2026-05-29-codex-port.md:170-180` - real path is docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-29-codex-port.md, and lines 170-180 do contain _LITERAL_SUBS as cited.
